ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a fusion (MFusion) that combines a generative MLP, a discriminative Elman RNN, and a simple lexical-structure rule to build semantic hierarchies — a pragmatic engineering combination rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. From my perspective as a theoretical rigourist, there is little formal justification of why this particular fusion should yield the reported gains, and key theoretical properties or ablations (e.g., why MLP vs other generative forms, or RNN vs other discriminators) are not emphasized. The application to a large Chinese Baidubaike corpus and the reported gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1) are useful, but the method largely composes known components and the language-independence claim is unsupported by cross-lingual validation, so the contribution reads as incremental rather than highly novel. | B(3): The paper presents a pragmatic fusion of existing components—a generative MLP, an Elman-style discriminative RNN, and a simple lexical-structure rule—to build semantic hierarchies from embeddings, and clearly contrasts results against many prior baselines (patterns, distributional, web-mining, embedding methods) on a large Baidubaike-trained setup. While the explicit fusion architecture and the reported gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1, and further improvement when combined with Wikipedia/CilinE) are useful and well-differentiated, the core models and the problem (hypernym/hyponym discovery) are well-studied and the contribution is mainly an empirical combination rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. From an empirical-pragmatist view this is a solid, reproducible incremental advance rather than a highly original breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a fusion (MFusion) that combines a generative MLP, a discriminative Elman RNN, and a simple lexical-structure rule to build semantic hierarchies — a pragmatic engineering combination rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. From my perspective as a theoretical rigourist, there is little formal justification of why this particular fusion should yield the reported gains, and key theoretical properties or ablations (e.g., why MLP vs other generative forms, or RNN vs other discriminators) are not emphasized. The application to a large Chinese Baidubaike corpus and the reported gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1) are useful, but the method largely composes known components and the language-independence claim is unsupported by cross-lingual validation, so the contribution reads as incremental rather than highly novel.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a pragmatic fusion of existing components—a generative MLP, an Elman-style discriminative RNN, and a simple lexical-structure rule—to build semantic hierarchies from embeddings, and clearly contrasts results against many prior baselines (patterns, distributional, web-mining, embedding methods) on a large Baidubaike-trained setup. While the explicit fusion architecture and the reported gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1, and further improvement when combined with Wikipedia/CilinE) are useful and well-differentiated, the core models and the problem (hypernym/hyponym discovery) are well-studied and the contribution is mainly an empirical combination rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. From an empirical-pragmatist view this is a solid, reproducible incremental advance rather than a highly original breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well organized and its main contributions (MFusion combining MLP, RNN and a lexical rule) are stated early and supported by experiments (precision/F1 numbers and comparisons to prior methods). Related work coverage is thorough and practical details such as hyperparameters and datasets are reported, which aids reproducibility. A few clarity shortcomings remain — e.g. the generative MLP training detail (trained only on positives) is mentioned but not fully explained, and the summary gives limited evidence about the clarity/effectiveness of figures and notation — hence a minor deduction. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (MFusion architecture, empirical gains, language-independence). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported and related work coverage is thorough, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear effective enough to support the claims, and writing is adequate, though a few procedural choices (e.g., training the generative MLP only on positives) and lack of a code release are described but could have been explained more transparently. From a practical, empirical perspective the presentation is solid with only minor clarity issues.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper appears well organized and its main contributions (MFusion combining MLP, RNN and a lexical rule) are stated early and supported by experiments (precision/F1 numbers and comparisons to prior methods). Related work coverage is thorough and practical details such as hyperparameters and datasets are reported, which aids reproducibility. A few clarity shortcomings remain — e.g. the generative MLP training detail (trained only on positives) is mentioned but not fully explained, and the summary gives limited evidence about the clarity/effectiveness of figures and notation — hence a minor deduction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (MFusion architecture, empirical gains, language-independence). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported and related work coverage is thorough, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear effective enough to support the claims, and writing is adequate, though a few procedural choices (e.g., training the generative MLP only on positives) and lack of a code release are described but could have been explained more transparently. From a practical, empirical perspective the presentation is solid with only minor clarity issues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares to a broad, appropriate set of baselines, uses the same labeled test set and standard metrics, and includes useful ablations and analysis, supporting largely fair benchmarking. However, the manuscript omits statistical significance testing and does not validate claims like language-independence or fully acknowledge cases where prior methods may match or exceed performance, so the comparison is strong but not completely comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of prior methods (pattern-based, distributional, web-mining, embeddings) and uses the same manually labeled test set and standard metrics, which supports fair benchmarking. It also includes ablations of constituent components (MLP, RNN, MLP+RNN) and reports gains from fusing with manual hierarchies, indicating the differences to baselines are examined. However, the manuscript appears to omit statistical significance testing and does not acknowledge potential parity or superior performance of any prior method in specific cases; additionally, the claim of language-independence is not validated cross-lingually. These omissions prevent a fully comprehensive comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares MFusion to a broad set of appropriate baselines (pattern-, distribution-, web-mining-, and embedding-based methods) and reports results on the same manually labeled test set used in prior work with matching precision/recall/F1 metrics, supporting fair conditions. It also ablates components (MLP, RNN, MLP+RNN) and analyzes limitations (failure cases, training detail), and discusses integration with existing hierarchies, indicating thorough coverage and clear distinction from competing approaches. From an empirical-pragmatic view this constitutes comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant prior work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents strong empirical results and appropriate baselines but share methodological concerns: no statistical significance testing, the generative MLP trained only on positive instances, and an unsupported language-independence claim since experiments are limited to Chinese. These gaps reduce confidence in the technical soundness and generality of the conclusions, so a moderate score is warranted despite promising results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and reports strong empirical numbers (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1) and component-level results (MMLPgen, MRNNdis, MMLP+RNN), but several methodological gaps weaken full technical validation. There is no mention of statistical significance or confidence intervals for reported metrics, the generative MLP was trained only on positive instances (a potentially biased training regime) and key theoretical justification for the fusion architecture is absent. The strong language-independence claim is unsupported because experiments are only on Chinese data and no cross-lingual validation is presented. Given these concerns (missing statistical testing, limited ablations/analysis, unsupported cross-lingual claim) the work is promising but not fully technically validated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims and supports them with extensive experiments on a large Baidubaike corpus and a held-out manually labeled test set, comparing against appropriate baselines and showing substantial absolute F1 gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1; hybrid with Wikipedia/CilinE → 82.01% F1). Mathematical content is minimal (no formal proofs needed) and appears internally consistent, but there are methodological gaps: the generative MLP was trained only on positives, no mention of statistical significance testing, and the language-independence claim is not empirically demonstrated beyond Chinese. Overall the experiments and baselines are strong and conclusions mostly justified, but the training-data choice and lack of some controls/statistics are minor concerns.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents solid empirical comparisons, component-level analyses, and transparent reporting of hyperparameters, but lacks statistical significance testing, systematic robustness/sensitivity analyses, cross-lingual validation, and public code for full reproducibility. Given the strong empirical contributions and clear acknowledgement of failure cases and limitations, but noting the missing rigor in robustness and reproducibility, a score of 4 appropriately reflects a good paper that falls short of excellence due to these omitted analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides solid empirical comparisons against multiple prior methods and reports component-level results (MMLPgen., MRNNdis., MMLP+RNN) which is helpful, but it omits key diagnostic rigor I require: no statistical significance or confidence intervals are reported, and cross-lingual validation is missing despite claiming language-independence. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is partial (some component combos shown) but lacks principled ablations (e.g., architecture vs. lexical rule, varied negative sampling, or projection analyses) and no code is released for exact reproducibility (though hyperparameters are reported). The authors do acknowledge some failure cases and a training limitation for the generative MLP, but overall the experimental depth is adequate yet missing important theory-driven and robustness analyses demanded for stronger claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid, empirically-driven experiments comparing MFusion against a wide set of baselines (patterns, distributions, web-mining, embeddings) and reports component-level results (MLP gen., RNN dis., MLP+RNN) plus gains from adding a lexical rule and manual hierarchies — supporting the main claims. Ablation on model components is present, but I see no systematic sensitivity analyses (e.g., to embedding size, training data size, or negative sampling) and no public code, which limits reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters and corpus details. Related work appears thoroughly covered, and the authors acknowledge specific failure cases and a key training limitation (MLP trained only on positives). Overall the evaluation is strong and pragmatic but misses some robustness/sensitivity experiments and full reproducibility details.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical problem and demonstrates strong empirical gains that make the approach likely to be adopted in applied systems. Criticisms about limited theoretical justification, insufficient ablation/statistical analysis, and single-language evaluation reduce its transformative potential, but the clear engineering advance and demonstrated utility justify a high impact score of 4 rather than a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (automatic construction of semantic hierarchies) and demonstrates strong empirical gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1 and further improvements when combined with Wikipedia/CilinE). However, from a theoretical-rigor perspective the work offers little formal justification of the proposed fusion architecture and lacks rigorous diagnostic ablations beyond the basic MLP/RNN components and no statistical significance or confidence intervals are reported. The language-independence claim is not substantiated by cross-lingual experiments (all experiments are on Chinese Baidubaike), which limits broader applicability. Overall, the contribution is useful and likely to be built on by practitioners but falls short of transformative impact due to limited theory and evaluation rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem (automatic hypernym/hyponym hierarchy construction) and demonstrates substantial empirical gains: MFusion achieves 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on a manually labeled test set, outperforming a broad set of prior pattern-, distribution-, web-mining- and embedding-based baselines. I value the pragmatic fusion of a generative MLP, discriminative RNN and a simple lexical rule, and the demonstrated benefit of augmenting with existing hierarchies (F1 rising to 82.01%), which makes the approach appealing and likely to be adopted or extended in applied systems. The work is not revolutionary theoretically, but offers a strong, reproducible engineering advance with clear practical utility and language-independence, so it should have solid influence in the area.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important NLP task with a solid empirical setup and shows substantial practical gains over strong baselines, making it appropriate for the community. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about limited theoretical justification, incomplete ablations, single-language evaluation, and lack of statistical testing are valid and temper a top score; a score of 4 reflects the paper's clear relevance and empirical contribution while acknowledging these methodological shortcomings that should be addressed for a top-tier acceptance.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (automatic construction of semantic hierarchies from embeddings) is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and the methodology (MLP + RNN fusion evaluated on a large Baidubaike corpus with standard precision/recall/F1 baselines) addresses the right audience. However, from a theoretical-rigor standpoint the paper lacks formal justification for the proposed fusion architecture and gives only partial ablations (reported MLP, RNN and MLP+RNN but no thorough diagnostic analyses of the lexical-structure rule or alternative fusion designs), and it asserts language-independence while evaluating exclusively on Chinese — no cross-lingual validation is provided. Finally, evaluation reports point estimates without stated significance testing or confidence intervals, which weakens its empirical claims for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP task (semantic hierarchy / hypernym discovery) using methods and evaluation practices expected at top venues: large-scale embeddings trained on Baidubaike, a manual test set shared with prior work, multiple strong baselines, and precision/recall/F1 reporting. The proposed MFusion combines complementary generative and discriminative models plus a simple lexical rule and shows substantial empirical gains (91.60% precision, 74.20% F1, and further improvement when merged with Wikipedia/CilinE), which aligns with my emphasis on pragmatic, reproducible systems that improve real tasks. The language-independence claim and clear comparisons to pattern-, distribution-, web-mining-, and embedding-based methods mean it is well positioned for the NLP/ML community.

main_claims: Proposes a fusion learning architecture (MFusion) that combines a generative MLP and a discriminative RNN plus a simple lexical-structure rule to construct semantic hierarchies from word embeddings.
MFusion achieves 91.60% precision and 74.20% F1 on the manually labeled test set, outperforming listed prior methods based on patterns, distributions, web mining and embeddings.
Combining MFusion with manually-built hierarchies (Wikipedia and CilinE) further improves F1 to 82.01%.
The proposed fusion learning architecture is language-independent.
_summary.methodology: Train Skip-gram word embeddings on a large Chinese encyclopedia (Baidubaike), then use an MLP as a generative model and an Elman-style RNN as a discriminative model, fuse their outputs and apply a simple lexical-structure rule to construct hypernym–hyponym hierarchies.
datasets: Manually labeled test dataset (same test dataset as Fu et al., 2014)
limitations: Fails to learn some hypernym–hyponym relations when the embedding representations of the pair are very close and a suitable projection cannot be found (example: 乌头属/Aconitum and 毛茛科/Ranunculaceae).
Generative MLP was trained only on positive instances (training procedure detail that restricts its training data).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic hierarchy construction / hypernym discovery

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Rigourist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a track record of publishing formal models of lexical semantics and learning theory.
traits: penalises papers that present engineering/architectural combinations without formal justification or theoretical analysis (subtracts points if no theory or formal properties are provided)
requires careful ablation and diagnostic experiments; deducts points if key components (e.g., MLP vs RNN vs fusion vs lexical rule) are not individually and comprehensively ablated
demands cross-lingual validation when claiming language-independence; will penalise strong claims of language-independence unless tested on at least one typologically different language
expects statistical significance testing and confidence intervals for reported metrics; downgrades papers that report point estimates only
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritise formal foundations, principled ablations, and rigorous claims — I reward clear theoretical contribution and penalise empirical-only papers that overclaim.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist with extensive experience building large-scale NLP systems and evaluating empirical methods for lexical resource construction and information extraction.
traits: rewards substantial empirical improvements on realistic, manually labeled test sets (gives bonus for large absolute gains over prior work)
values pragmatic, reproducible engineering solutions that combine complementary models and simple rules (awards points for clear, implementable architectures and gains from simple heuristics)
credits demonstrations of improvement when integrating learned models with existing lexical resources (favourably scores hybrid systems that boost practical utility)
prefers strong experimental methodology (large training corpora, clear baselines, and real-world datasets) and will be generous if these are present even without formal proofs
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I focus on practical impact, reproducibility, and empirical gains — I reward well-executed empirical work that meaningfully improves real tasks and resources.
